text: Upon entering through Palmer's point, the surgeon observes extensive omental adhesions covering the anterior abdominal wall and a pulled-up intestinal loop. Adhesiolysis is performed using a harmonic scalpel and an atraumatic intestinal grasper, effectively separating the adhesions with ultrasound vibrations.

The laparoscopic view shows the surgeon inspecting the small intestine for inter-bowel loop adhesions. The harmonic device is used for adhesiolysis where the omentum is adhered, while scissors and sharp cold dissection are employed for adhesions between bowel serosa. The inferior epigastric vessel is visible above, requiring careful manipulation to avoid contact with the harmonic tip. The medial umbilical ligament is also visible in the surgical field.

Following the completion of omental adhesiolysis, the surgeon identifies multiple adhesion bands between the bowel loops and the omentum, which are contributing to the obstruction. The surgeon begins to separate the omentum from the bowel loops using surgical scissors.
The surgeon uses instruments to carefully separate two loops of the small intestine that are adhered together, ensuring they are distinct and free from each other.

The surgeon carefully separates serosal adhesions between the small bowel loops using cold scissors, avoiding energy devices to prevent damage. The anatomy is meticulously preserved as adhesions and bands are resolved, with the Palmer's point entry providing a panoramic view of the small intestine.

The surgeon carefully performs adhesiolysis on the small bowel using a 30-degree telescope for enhanced visualization. The light cable is rotated to provide a comprehensive view of the bands to be cut. A structure resembling a vascular pedicle is identified and separated using a harmonic device to minimize the risk of bleeding.
The surgeon uses a traumatic intestinal grasper with the left hand to hold the small intestine, ensuring no trauma is inflicted on the tissue. The right hand holds scissors, ready for dissection.

The surgeon identifies additional bands of omentum and notes features of hepatitis in the patient. Near the hepatic flexure of the colon, a band is visible connecting to the cecum, which is necessary to cut.

The surgeon identifies adhesions and a herniated bowel loop within the small intestine, which are separated to relieve subacute obstruction. A band holding multiple bowel loops together is visible, along with another band from the sigmoid colon adhering to the small bowel. The surgeon uses a harmonic device to cut the tight band connecting the sigmoid colon to the small bowel.
The surgeon applies entromedial traction to the small intestine and uses a harmonic scalpel to cut a band causing obstruction, ensuring the release of intestinal serosa.

The surgeon separates a lump and opens it to straighten the intestinal loops, releasing obstructions. An omental band is also separated to prevent inter-bowel loop adhesion. The surgeon performs thorough suction and lavage, introducing two to three liters of saline to wash out the small bowel. The intestine loops are free, allowing for suction irrigation with shaking movements to wash out oozing. Fluid is repeatedly sucked from the pelvis, visible near the bladder. Three liters of crystalloid solution are left in the abdomen to keep the intestines floating and prevent further adhesion.

The surgical field shows no visible adhesions, with the omentum and bowel loops now free. The surgeon performs suction to remove fluid, positioning the patient's head upwards to facilitate drainage into the pelvis. The abdomen is repeatedly irrigated with Ringer's lactate, and fluids are suctioned out. The final suction is performed to ensure the abdomen is clean before leaving crystalloid solution.
The surgeon introduces a clear fluid into the abdominal cavity, ensuring there is no visible blood present.
The surgical field shows clear fluid without any visible reduced material. No drain is placed to retain the fluid within the abdominal cavity, ensuring the intestines remain suspended in the fluid.

The liver and gallbladder are visibly submerged in crystallite solution within the abdominal cavity. The surgeon ensures the patient remains in a neutral position to prevent complications such as perforation following adhesiolysis.
